## CHAPTER 11: “NEAR COLLAPSE” — REFINED DRAFT (MODE B)

The aftershock hits during Pouring—one of the long, rolling ones that makes whole districts feel slightly unfastened from the world.

Jhace feels it as a low, sick twist through stone before anyone above notices. By the time the surface gauges start to chatter, he’s already running.

Junction three: the one feeding half the mid-district shelters.

The housing leans barely off true, but the hum in the mains is wrong—strained, high-pitched, like wire pulled past tolerance. Copper-gold dust lingers in seams from previous work, a faint reminder of how far he’s already pushed his body.

“Evacuation status?” he calls as he reaches the platform.

“Not complete,” a Guild runner answers. “Two dozen still moving up from the Underface chambers. We need another—”

The junction shudders. The runner grabs a handhold, swears, and sprints back toward the stairwell to hurry people along.

Jhace steps to the housing and lays both hands against corroded metal.

The vibration under his palms is all wrong: load stacking along one side, pressure trying to shear the housing off its mounts. The lattice reinforcements they installed took most of the strain, but the aftershock has found a new angle the design didn’t predict.

*We should have recalculated that node. We didn’t. That’s on me.*

He should wait for Tiffani. They have protocols now: assess, confer, choose the least destructive option.

Another tremor rolls through the junction. The housing shifts half a handspan. Somewhere below, a child cries out in fear.

Jhace stops thinking.

He pushes.

Wholeness-bias floods from chest to shoulder to copper-gold etched palms, pouring into failing metal. Every microfracture lights up under his inner sense, familiar as breath. He clamps down on all of them at once.

The housing steadies.

His tremor spikes instantly—Stage 2 baseline tearing open into Stage 3 overload. Hands shaking so hard his grip leaves stuttering smears of light along the casing.

*Hold. Just hold. Give them time to clear.*

He locks his jaw, muscles burning. Cost screams through him, but the junction stays upright.

Voices echo from the Underface stairwell.

“Move, move—keep the line!”

“Don’t push—there’s room—”

The aftershock doesn’t care. Another wave hits, sharper this time. The mains groan. Pressure climbs. The strain wants somewhere to go, and right now that somewhere is *through him*.

His vision tunnels. The world narrows to the vibrating metal under his hands and the roaring of blood in his ears. Breath shortens to ragged sips.

Stage 3, deep. He knows the feel now—muscles firing out of sequence, nerves misfiring, body starting to ignore instructions.

He holds anyway.

*We said we’d decide together. I said I’d wait. I lied.*

Stone shifts behind him. Footsteps—uneven, familiar.

Tiffani.

“Don’t,” he manages.

She ignores that. She always ignores the wrong things.

She takes in the situation at a glance: his stance, the way the housing wants to peel off its mounts, the pitch of the hum, the shudder in his shoulders.

“How many still below?” she demands.

“Half,” he says. Or thinks he says. It comes out a cracked whisper.

She looks toward the stairwell, calculates velocity of movement versus remaining clearance. Angles. Probabilities.

Then she moves to him. Not to the housing. To *him*.

She sets her hands on top of his. Her grip is firm enough to hurt.

“Jhace.” Command voice. Flat. No room to wedge argument into. “Drop.”

He doesn’t.

Can’t.

If he lets go, the housing shifts. If it shifts, the mains shear. If the mains shear while people are still in the chambers—

He shakes his head, jaw clamped.

“Drop it,” she repeats. “Trust me.”

The words cut through the panic in a way nothing else has. Not because of what they mean, but because of what they assume: that she’s already running calculations faster than his fear.

He drags his eyes to hers. The edges of her face blur, but her gaze is sharp.

“Now,” she says.

He lets go.

The housing lurches. For a heartbeat it feels like the entire junction will rip free and crash sideways into the access stair.

Tiffani’s Soulpulse engages before the movement completes.

Form-bias snaps along precise internal lines she’s mapped a thousand times in her head. Copper-gold arcs through selected mounts, not to heal them, but to redirect force: up one brace, down into reinforced footings, away from the sheer line.

The junction slams back into alignment. Not perfect. But not falling.

She holds. Thirty, maybe forty heartbeats. Long enough for the last of the evacuees to spill up and out. Long enough for Guild crews to jam emergency struts under the housing and lock them in.

“Bracing secure!” someone shouts. “You can release!”

Tiffani lets go.

The mains settle onto iron instead of flesh.

Jhace’s knees go out.

He hits the platform hard, rolls to one side, curls around the shaking that has nowhere left to go. Every muscle spasms. His hands clamp and unclamp without his permission. Stage 3 has him by the spine and is pulling.

Tiffani drops down beside him. She catches his wrists before he can slam his hands against the stone in useless protest.

“Breathe,” she says. “Four counts in.”

He gasps, manages two before his chest seizes.

“Again.” Sharper. “Four.”

One. Two. Three. Four.

“Hold.”

His body doesn’t want to. He does it anyway.

“Four counts out.”

Air leaves in a broken rush.

They repeat the cycle until the edge of the shaking blunts from catastrophic to constant. His muscles still twitch. His fingers still tremble. But the sense of imminent system failure eases half a degree.

She doesn’t let go of his wrists.

“You broke protocol,” she says at last.

He closes his eyes. “I know.”

“We said we’d decide together before you tried to hold something that big.”

“There wasn’t time.”

“There was.” No heat. Just steel. “You chose not to use it.”

“I heard them below,” he says. Voice low. “If I’d waited, and it failed…” He swallows. “I’ve already watched too many people die while I calculated what I should do.”

“And you’ll watch more,” she answers. “We both will. That isn’t failure. That’s limitation.” A beat. “This was you refusing to accept limitation.”

He doesn’t argue. The tremor under her fingers is answer enough.

“You almost pushed to Stage 4,” she adds quietly. “Is that cost acceptable to you?”

“If they’d lived either way? No.” He forces his hand open. His fingers don’t fully obey. “If this was the only way? Yes.”

“It wasn’t.” Her jaw tightens. “You decided that before you gave me a chance to prove it.”

Silence stretches. Not empty—dense with anger, fear, and something worse: recognition.

Guild crews finish bolting the emergency struts into the platform. The junction hum settles into something close to normal. Evacuees sit in huddled clusters at the far end of the chamber, whispering, eyes wide.

“They’re alive,” he says.

“Yes.”

“I’m not collapsed.”

“Not this time.”

He lets out a rough, humorless breath. “You going to say ‘I told you so’?”

“No.” She shifts to sit with her back against the housing, shoulders nearly touching his. “I’m going to say we tighten the protocols.”

He snorts. “Tighter than ‘don’t hit Stage 3 unless we both agree’?”

“Yes. New rule.” She tilts her head toward him. “If I tell you to drop, you drop. No argument. No heroics. No negotiation.”

He stares at the opposite wall. A hairline crack runs from floor to knee height, a small reminder of how much they almost lost. “That’s a lot of trust.”

“Yes,” she says. “It is.”

“And if you’re wrong?”

“Then I carry that cost.” She keeps her hands loosely around his wrists. “You trusted me with the junction. I’m asking you to trust me with you.”

He hates how much he wants to. Hates how much of him already does.

“Okay,” he says finally. “If you say drop, I drop.”

She nods once. “Good.”

They sit there until his breathing evens out and his hands shake at a level that, for him, counts as stable. Tiffani only releases his wrists when she’s certain he won’t try to stand too fast and fall on his face.

When they do stand, every joint complains. His legs feel made of slag. Her back cracks when she straightens. The kind of shared ache that says they’ve survived something they probably shouldn’t have.

“We should rest,” she says.

“Probably,” he agrees.

They don’t.

They move together along the junction corridor, checking the new struts, verifying bolt placement, confirming that the thing they nearly died holding will stand on its own.

Only when every connection reads solid to both of them does Tiffani step back.

“Next time,” she says, “we act before it gets this bad. Together.”

“Together,” he echoes.

The aftershock sequence fades into distant memory. The junction hums. People above breathe easier without knowing why.

Jhace’s hands are still shaking.

But he didn’t break.

And he’s no longer pretending he has to be the only thing holding everything up.

# CHAPTER 11: "NEAR COLLAPSE" — MODE B REFINED DRAFT

The aftershock arrives during Pouring phase—a directional jolt transmitted along stone and metal. Not the worst since the Piercing, but misaligned enough to push strained junction housings past tolerance.

Three streets south of the coordination point, Jhace feels the load shift through the grid before he hears anything. Tension rising in the bolts. Pressure stacking wrong across already-fatigued struts.

The junction housing ahead lists with a slow, deliberate groan.

Civilians are still inside the adjacent shelter—forty-seven people waiting on clearance. If the junction fails, the cascade takes the corridor, the walkway, the shelter. Everything tied into this mid-district segment.

He moves without calculation.

His palms hit corroded iron. Copper-gold builds too fast—undirected Wholeness-bias saturating every fracture at once. The tremor spikes immediately. Not the fine Stage 2 quiver he's learned to work around, but deep, violent shaking that climbs into his arms in a heartbeat.

He pushes anyway. Forcing load back into the housing.

The structure steadies.

Slightly.

Enough to hold.

But releasing now sends the load into uncontrolled slide.

His breath stutters. Sweat drops onto metal. Vision narrows. The tremor climbs into his shoulders, tightens his jaw, locks his posture in place.

He's trapped in his own hold.

Civilians are still moving. Don't release.

Tiffani would calculate the release sequence. Tiffani would find three anchor points.

But she isn't here.

Copper-gold spreads up his forearms—new permanent etchings forming as Stage 3 settles in, slow and burning.

***

Tiffani feels the shift before she rounds the last corner.

Jhace's Resonance signature—usually clean, steady—spikes into distortion, rippling through metal in a pattern she's learned to read with unwelcome familiarity.

She runs.

Her leg brace catches on uneven stone. Pain signals fire sharp and immediate, but she files them away and continues.

She reaches the junction as his knees begin to give.

He's still holding. Barely. His hands move in uncontrolled pulses, each tremor mirrored by sympathetic vibration in the housing he's bracing.

She doesn't engage her own Soulpulse. Not yet. Adding a second pattern too early breaks the fragile stability he's maintaining.

Instead, she covers his hands with hers—skin on skin, copper traces touching copper.

"Drop," she says.

He doesn't respond.

She tightens her grip around his wrists. "Drop it. Trust me."

His eyes snap toward her. Recognition—faint, flickering.

"Now."

He releases.

The structure tilts. A half-breath from failure—

—and she catches it with Form-bias activated clean and sharply defined. Three anchor points: upper seam, rear plate, where bracing timber meets fatigued stone. Load redistributes across exactly those coordinates.

The housing steadies.

Guild emergency crew slides bracing beams into place.

"Secure," one calls.

She releases.

Both collapse back against the nearest wall.

Jhace's tremor is full-body now—uncontrolled firing along every muscle group. Stage 3. Deep. Possibly brushing Stage 4 edge.

Tiffani takes his wrists in both hands. "Four counts in."

He inhales—unsteady, uneven. But four counts.

"Hold."

He holds.

"Four counts out."

They repeat the sequence until his breathing stabilizes, though the tremor remains severe.

She doesn't let go.

"You almost died," she says.

He swallows. "But I didn't."

"Because I told you to let go." She meets his eyes. "Next time, you release before I have to order it."

He nods once.

The junction crew continues securing the housing. Civilians file past in controlled lines, moving toward reinforced shelters.

Jhace studies his hands. Copper-gold spreads from palm to elbow—new patterns, sharp and unmistakable. His baseline tremor doesn't ease.

"I violated our agreement," he says quietly. "We negotiate before crisis."

"Yes."

"I'm sorry."

"Sorry isn't a solution." Her voice stays even. "Understanding is."

He waits.

"You moved without calculation," she says. "Because calculation means evaluating acceptable loss. And you can't."

He doesn't deny it.

"That will kill you," she continues. "Not immediately. But soon."

"I know."

"And when it kills you, I'll be the one holding the line you dropped."

Still no answer.

She threads her fingers through his shaking ones—an anchor, not comfort. "We are load-bearing systems. Remove one support, the other fails. That's not metaphor. That's structure."

"I know."

"Then act like you know."

Silence holds between them—steady, heavy, necessary.

He shifts his gaze toward the braced junction. "Thank you for coming."

"I always will," she says. "But I'd prefer not to run through aftershocks because you won't wait for support."

A faint exhale. "Noted."

"Is it?"

His eyes meet hers. "Yes. I'll try to wait."

"Try is insufficient."

"It's what I have."

She evaluates that honestly. Probability of behavioral shift: low. But partnership isn't optimization. It's reinforcement—structural, deliberate.

"Then we build protocols," she says. "If I'm not there, you hold for a set duration. Then release. No negotiation with yourself."

"Civilians might die."

"Civilians will die if you collapse and can't respond to the next failure."

He absorbs the logic.

Finally: "Okay. We build protocols."

She nods. "Good."

They stand. Movement hurts. Both are cost-marked, both drained, both still operating out of necessity rather than readiness.

Jhace flexes his hands. The tremor remains—constant, deep. A new baseline.

"We should rest," Tiffani says.

"We should," he echoes. "We won't."

"I know."

They move toward the next site. Together. Because this is the work: continuing despite cost, stabilizing what can be stabilized, learning how not to collapse under each other's weight.

Behind them, the junction stands fully braced—not by Soulpulse, but by correct engineering and coordinated work.

That distinction, they both know, is the one that prevents failure in the long term.

# CHAPTER 11: “NEAR COLLAPSE” — DRAFT 3 CLEAN (HYBRID)

The aftershock arrives during Pouring phase—a long, directional jolt transmitted along stone and metal. Not the strongest since the Piercing, but misaligned enough to shove strained junction housings past tolerance.

Three streets south of the main coordination point, Jhace feels the load shift through the grid before the surface gauges twitch.

Tension climbs in the bolts. Pressure stacks wrong along already-fatigued struts.

The junction housing ahead leans with a slow, deliberate groan.

Civilians are still inside the adjacent shelter—forty-seven people waiting on clearance. If the junction fails, the cascade takes the corridor, the walkway, the shelter. Everything tied into this mid-district segment.

He moves before he finishes the thought.

His palms slap corroded iron. Copper-gold builds too fast—undirected Wholeness-bias flooding every fracture at once. The tremor spikes instantly. Not the fine Stage 2 quiver he’s learned to work around, but deep, violent shaking that climbs into his arms in a heartbeat.

He pushes anyway.

Pressure drives back into the housing. Microfractures light up along his inner sense. He clamps all of them at once, brute-forcing the entire joint toward stability.

The structure steadies.

Slightly.

Enough to hold.

But releasing now would send the load into uncontrolled slide.

Breath rasps. Sweat drops onto metal. Vision narrows until all that exists is the shuddering housing under his hands and the high, strained note in the mains.

He’s trapped in his own hold.

Below, voices echo through the shelter access.

“Keep moving—don’t stop in the corridor!”

“Lift her—watch the step—”

If he lets go too early, the whole system comes down on them.

*Tiffani would calculate the release sequence. Three anchor points. A clean redirect.*

Tiffani isn’t here.

Copper-gold spreads up his forearms—new marks burning into skin as Stage 3 settles in, slow and hot.

Another wave of aftershock rolls through. The mains groan. The housing wants to twist off its mounts.

He digs in harder.

*Hold. Just hold. Just long enough.*

His shoulders shake under the strain. Fingers cramp. His jaw locks until his teeth ache. Every muscle screams to stop.

He doesn’t.

***

Tiffani feels the distortion before she sees the junction.

Jhace’s Resonance signature—usually tight and clean, even when strained—spikes into a ragged pattern that rips through the metal under her feet. Wrong rhythm. Wrong amplitude. Too much.

She runs.

Stone underfoot shudders with each aftershock pulse. Her leg, still not fully reconciled with impact and splint and work, lights with pain on every uneven patch. She acknowledges it, categorizes it, discards it.

She rounds the last corner as his knees start to give.

He’s braced against the junction housing, hands burning copper-gold, shoulders locked. The tremor running through him is so violent the metal vibrates in sympathy.

If she adds her Soulpulse now, she breaks the fragile balance he’s carved out of brute force.

So she doesn’t.

She steps in behind him, covers his hands with hers—skin on skin on iron, her cooler grip wrapping his shaking fingers.

“Drop,” she says.

He doesn’t.

He can’t see the evacuees still in the stairwell from here, but he can hear them; every footfall slaps against his nerves. If he lets go and the housing fails while they’re still below—

His head jerks once in refusal.

She tightens her hold on his wrists. “Drop it. Trust me.”

His eyes snap to hers. Recognition flickers, a thin line under the wash of pain and fear.

“Now,” she says. No space for argument.

He lets go.

The structure lurches. For half a breath, the housing tears sideways, metal screaming, the whole junction sliding toward catastrophic failure—

—and her Form-bias snaps into place.

Copper-gold arcs through three precise points: upper seam, rear plate, the junction where bracing timber meets fatigued stone. Force redirects up one brace and down into reinforced footings, away from the sheer line.

The housing slams back into alignment. Not perfect. But not falling.

She holds. Twenty, thirty heartbeats—long enough for the last of the evacuees to spill out of the stairwell in a stumbling line, guided by Guild runners shouting for them to clear the platform.

Emergency crews throw struts into place, sliding heavy timber under the housing, bolting metal braces to mounts they can now safely reach.

“Bracing secure!” someone calls. “You can release!”

She lets go.

The mains settle onto iron instead of flesh.

Jhace’s legs buckle.

He drops to one knee, then all the way down, curling around the shaking that rips through him with nowhere left to go. His hands clench and splay, fingers spasming uncontrollably. Tremor storms up his arms and into his shoulders—Stage 3, deep, brushing the edge of something worse.

Tiffani goes down with him, not gracefully. She catches both his wrists before he can smack his hands against stone.

“Breathe,” she says. “Four counts in.”

He drags air in—one, two—stumble—three, four.

“Hold.”

His body hates it. He does it anyway.

“Four counts out.”

Air leaves in a broken rush.

Again.

Four in. Hold. Four out.

They repeat the cycle until his breathing finds a rhythm that isn’t pure survival. The shaking eases from catastrophic to constant: still Stage 3, still bad, but no longer actively trying to tear him apart from the inside.

She doesn’t let go.

“You almost died,” she says.

His throat works. “But I didn’t.”

“Because I told you to let go.” Her voice stays even. “Next time, you release before I have to order it.”

His gaze drops to her hands wrapped around his. Copper-gold marks echo from his skin to hers. “I broke the protocols,” he says. “We said we’d decide together before I tried to hold something that big.”

“Yes.”

“I’m—”

“Sorry isn’t a solution.” No sharpness. Just assessment. “Understanding is.”

He shuts his mouth. Waits.

“You moved without calculation,” she says. “Because calculation means evaluating acceptable loss. And you can’t.”

He doesn’t argue. The tremor under her fingers is admission.

“That will kill you,” she continues quietly. “Maybe not in this Tide. But soon.”

“I know.”

“And when it does, I’ll be the one standing here holding the line you dropped, without you. Explain how that’s better than waiting three breaths for me to get here.”

He has no answer for that.

She threads her fingers through his shaking ones—not comfort, but anchor. “We are load-bearing systems. Remove one support, the other fails. That isn’t metaphor. That’s structure.”

“I know,” he says.

“Then act like you know.”

Silence sits with them—heavy, correct.

On the far side of the platform, evacuees huddle in loose groups. A child laughs once, high and startled, the sound of someone realizing they’re still alive.

Jhace looks toward the braced housing. Fresh struts, new bolts. The junction hum has settled back into something close to normal.

“Thank you for coming,” he says.

“I always will.” She shifts her back against the wall, shoulders nearly brushing his. “But I’d rather not sprint through aftershocks because you won’t wait for support.”

A huff of air escapes him—halfway to a laugh, broken on the edges. “Noted.”

“Is it?”

He meets her eyes. “Yes. I’ll… try to wait.”

“‘Try’ is insufficient.”

“It’s what I have,” he says honestly.

She weighs that. Probability of perfect compliance: low. Probability of incremental change: non-zero.

“Then we build stronger protocols,” she says. “If I’m not there, you hold for a fixed count. Then you release. No negotiation with yourself.”

“Civilians might die,” he says.

“Civilians will die if you push into Stage 4 and can’t respond to anything else.” She doesn’t look away. “Your martyr reflex doesn’t save more people. It just kills you slower.”

He absorbs it, each word another weight_slotting into place.

“Okay,” he says finally. “We build protocols.”

“Good.”

They stay on the floor until his muscles stop trying to rebel against existing. When he stands, his legs feel like slag pulled from cooling troughs. When she stands, her spine pops and her thigh complains about the sprint she forced through old damage.

“We should rest,” she says.

“We should,” he agrees. “We won’t.”

“I know.”

They move together along the platform, checking each new strut, each bolted joint, each connection where metal meets stone. They don’t leave until both of them agree the junction will now stand on its own.

Only then does Tiffani step back.

“Next time,” she says, “we act before it gets this bad. Together.”

“Together,” he echoes.

The aftershock sequence moves on to bother some other district. The junction hums steady. People above walk streets they don’t realize nearly failed.

Jhace’s hands still shake.

But he didn’t break.

And he’s no longer pretending he has to be the only thing holding everything up.

# CHAPTER 12: “HOLDING TOGETHER” — MODE B REFINED DRAFT

Two Tides after the near-collapse, Saint Merro hums in a lower key.

Not emergency noise now—no running crews, no constant shouting—just the steady clatter of careful reconstruction. Hammers. Measured voices. Carts moving slow over repaired seams.

Jhace sits on the forge overlook wall, back against warm stone, hands resting palm-up on his knees. The tremor moves through his fingers in continuous waves—Stage 3 baseline now, no longer dipping back toward stillness even in rest. Copper-gold tracery has spread from his palms to his forearms, etched in lines that catch Quench light.

Footsteps approach. Even gait, but with the faintest hitch he’s learned to recognize.

Tiffani.

She settles beside him without asking, setting two cups of water and a cloth-wrapped bundle between them. Practical offerings: hydration, food, not comfort. He takes a cup. Drinks. The water tastes of mineral and heat.

Below, reinforcement crews finish securing a new lattice along one of the mid-corridor junction runs—seven braces in overlapping pattern, all angles and arcs. The design is hers. The implementation is theirs.

“They finished junction four today,” she says, watching the crew check the last bolts. “No deviation from projected stress path.”

“Any complaints?” His voice scrapes around exhaustion.

“Only from supervisors who dislike being told their previous models were insufficient.” A small exhale, almost humor. “They’ll adjust.”

Silence stretches between them. Not awkward—just full.

She unwraps the bundle. Bread. A small wedge of hard cheese. She breaks the bread in half, hands him the larger piece. He doesn’t object. She’s already calculated who needs more.

“There’s something I need to tell you,” she says.

He waits.

“Before the Piercing,” she continues, “I worked glasshouse design on the eastern rim. Custom observatories. Thermal balances. Stress tests.” Her fingers flatten crumbs on her knee, aligning them into a line, then dispersing them again. “We were testing a new panel configuration. Larger panes. Different alloy ratio.”

He’s heard this referred to in fragments from others. Never from her.

“My friend Marisse was on the platform,” Tiffani says. “We ran through the numbers. I calculated the stress distribution across the frame. Triple-checked the geometry.” A precise inhale. “I told her it was safe.”

Quench light catches along the copper dust still embedded in the stone around them.

“The third pane shattered on heat swell,” she says. “Thermal expansion on that batch was higher than the standard coefficient. I used what the Guild tables gave me. I should have measured our specific glass. That’s the variable that mattered.”

She doesn’t raise her voice. She doesn’t look away.

“Pane fractured,” she continues. “Support rails twisted. The whole top segment came down. Marisse took most of it.” A beat. “She bled out before healers made the climb.”

He doesn’t interrupt with “I’m sorry.” Pity would be insult here. He waits.

“I recalculated that configuration hundreds of times after,” she says. “The error is always the same. The number I trusted was wrong.” Her jaw tightens. “So now I measure everything. Twice. Three times. I never accept a table value without verification.”

He watches her hands. They don’t tremble like his, but there’s tension in them—fine and constant.

“How old were you?” he asks.

“Nineteen Tides.” A corner of her mouth twitches. “Young enough to be forgiven. Old enough that I don’t.”

He nods once. “Apprentice on an advanced commission.”

“Yes.”

He turns his gaze back to the junction lattice below. Lets the quiet sit a few breaths before he offers his own.

“I was seven,” he says. “Underface, midline. My parents were maintenance crew.” His throat tightens, but the words come anyway. He’s rehearsed this story silently for Turns; saying it aloud feels like stepping out onto unfinished scaffolding.

“I was in the street above their sector,” he goes on. “Playing near an access hatch I wasn’t supposed to touch.” The memory resurfaces in precise detail—stone under bare feet, the smell of damp metal, the hum of the grid steady and comforting.

“I heard a crack,” he says. “Not loud. Just… wrong. Beam shifting under strain.” His fingers curl, phantom sensation of that sound moving through bone. “I thought about shouting. Or running to the foreman. But I wasn’t sure. And if I was wrong, they’d say I was being dramatic. Or making excuses to avoid going home when the shift ended.”

He inhales. Lets it out slowly.

“So I stayed quiet,” he says. “Ten breaths later, the support failed. Their section came down.”

He remembers the dust plume punching up through the street seams. The sudden silence after. The way the grid hum dropped two notes before resetting around the gap where their weight used to be.

“Everyone told me it wasn’t my fault,” he says. “I was a child. I didn’t have training. I couldn’t have known.”

“But you believe you could have,” Tiffani says softly.

He nods. “I heard it. They died. The equation my mind writes is simple.”

She’s quiet a moment. Watching the junction below, where workers are stowing tools.

“We will spend our lives believing lies about what we could have prevented,” she says eventually. “You had no data. I had wrong data. The result is the same: guilt that doesn’t care whether its premise is sound.”

He huffs a humorless breath. “You’re calling my guilt bad math.”

“I’m calling mine bad math,” she corrects. “Yours mirrors it. Cleanly.”

He turns that over. The thought sits uncomfortably right.

“So what then?” he asks. “We just live with it?”

“We live with it,” she says. “Or we choose a different reference point.”

He glances at her. “Which is?”

“Each other.”

He blinks. Waits.

“You believe you should have warned them,” she says. “I believe you were a child who heard something you couldn’t interpret yet. I believe your silence didn’t kill them.”

“You weren’t there.”

“I don’t need to be. I have the structure.” Her gaze is steady. “You believe you killed them. I believe you didn’t.”

He swallows. “And Marisse?”

“I believe I made a wrong call,” she says without flinching. “I also believe the Guild tables were flawed. Both are true. If you told me it was only my fault, I’d know *that* was bad math.”

“So we trade,” he says slowly. “You hold the equation I can’t see straight. I hold yours.”

“That,” she says, “is load redistribution.”

Down below, the crew chief signals completion—two short bell strikes. Junction four’s lattice is officially in service.

Tiffani pulls her slate into her lap. The surface is already marked with lines and arcs. She smooths one section with the side of her thumb, clears enough space to draw fresh.

“Next phase,” she says. “Seven junctions total tied into one reinforcement scheme. If we stagger the work, we can reach all of them before the next major aftershock cycle.”

She sketches quickly—nodes, paths, stress arrows.

“We can’t do every failure,” she continues. “So we choose the ones that underpin the most secondary structures. These seven carry the biggest consequences.”

He leans closer, following her lines. “You’re building redundancy,” he says. “If one joint fails, the others pick up the load.”

“Distributed risk,” she says. “Same principle we just agreed to apply to guilt.”

He studies the pattern. “Where do you need me?”

She taps three junctions on the slate. “Your Wholeness for these. They’re cracked through old repair seams. I’ll take these”—she marks two with a different symbol—“where geometry is the bigger problem. We overlap here and here.” She circles intersecting nodes. “Redundancy. Neither of us is single point of failure.”

He nods. “How long?”

“Two Anneals at minimum capacity,” she says. “Three if we respect the protocols we swore we’d follow.”

“Three, then,” he says.

She glances up, measuring whether he means it.

“Three,” he repeats.

Below, a familiar figure crosses the junction. Chloen. Her gait is even now—no limp, no brace. She carries a bundle of tools over one shoulder and stops to check the new lattice, running an appreciative hand along a fresh brace before moving on.

Tiffani watches her go. “You saved her,” she says.

“We saved her,” he answers automatically.

She allows that. “We save what we can,” she says. “We fail where we must. We miscalculate. We overextend. We pay.”

He looks at his hands. At the constant tremor. At the copper-gold lines that will never fully fade.

“Then we pay together,” he says.

She nods once. “Together.”

Quench deepens; lanterns bloom across the mid-district. Workers disperse toward food and rest. The newly braced junction hums steady—one component in a network they’re slowly, deliberately teaching to hold.

They sit a few breaths longer.

Then Tiffani folds the slate. “We have appointments with junction five in the next Tide. We should rest while rest is structurally available.”

“Structurally available,” he echoes, mouth quirking. “Not emotionally comfortable.”

“Emotional comfort is not one of our design constraints,” she says.

He huffs a faint laugh. “No. It isn’t.”

They stand. His tremor shakes the last crumbs from his fingers. Her leg catches once on the step down, but she adjusts before he can reach.

They descend from the overlook into a city that is still broken, still dangerous, still theirs to hold where they can.

Not alone.

Never again alone, if they can help it.

Together, they walk toward whatever still needs bracing.

# CHAPTER 12: "HOLDING TOGETHER" — MODE B REFINED DRAFT

Two Tides after the junction collapse, Quench phase settles across the district with the soft hum of cooling metal. Reconstruction crews secure lattice segments along the mid-corridor—methodical work punctuated by the ring of hammers on iron. Heat rises in low waves from fresh welds. The air tastes of stone dust and forge-smoke.

Jhace sits on the overlook above the rebuilt forge. His hands rest on his knees, tremor constant—Stage 3 baseline now, a new permanent rhythm etched into nerve and bone. Copper-gold tracings mark skin from palms to elbows, bright even without active Resonance.

Tiffani approaches carrying water and wrapped bread. She offers the cup; he drinks. No words yet. She sits beside him, both watching crews secure another reinforcement arc below.

Silence holds long enough to become acknowledgment.

"We're planning the next phase," she says finally. "Seven junction points. Distributed load. Two Anneals if we execute correctly."

He nods once. The tremor makes the motion uneven.

"Authorities want oversight," she adds. "We'll document everything, but we keep operational control."

Another nod. Smaller.

She studies his hands—no judgment, no pity. Just assessment.

"There's something you need to know," she says.

He waits.

"Marisse. My friend. The glasshouse accident."

Jhace turns slightly toward her.

"We were testing a new panel configuration. I ran the calculations. Checked them. Triple-checked." Her breath stays even, disciplined. "The third pane failed. Thermal expansion. I used the standard coefficient. Didn't measure batch variance."

Her gaze stays on the district below. "The shards cut deep. She didn't survive."

Jhace doesn't fill the quiet.

"I recalculated that configuration hundreds of times," she continues. "The error was mine. I missed a variable that should have been measured."

He sits with that weight.

Then: "You were nineteen Turns."

"Yes."

"Apprentice level."

"Yes."

"Working beyond required mastery."

"Yes."

He flexes his hands—tremor sharp for a breath. "I was seven when my parents died. Underface collapse during maintenance. I was outside. Heard the support crack." His breath tightens. "I hesitated. Didn't call out. Afraid of being wrong." A pause. "They died inside."

Tiffani shifts—slight, deliberate. Not closer, just aligned.

"Children aren't responsible for structural failures they can't assess," she says.

He shakes his head once. "Logic doesn't change the story I built. My fear killed them. That's the equation that stayed."

"That's a lie."

"Yes." He meets her eyes. "But believing it is easier than letting it go."

Tiffani nods slowly. "We will spend our lives believing lies about things we couldn't control."

He looks back toward the workers below. "Unless we believe each other instead."

She takes out her slate, unfolds the lattice design. Chalk marks show stress paths, redistribution arcs. "This will work. Forty percent cost reduction if we execute correctly."

He traces one chalk line with a fingertip. "Only works if you're part of it."

"I will be." She taps three critical nodes. "You take these. I take these. Redundancy here."

He studies the configuration. "Agreed."

They refine the plan. Chalk adjusts. Load paths correct. Quench hum settles around them like the city breathing.

Below, Chloen passes carrying timber for the next bracing set. Her gait is steady—no limp. The memory of her Piercing collapse folds into the present without interrupting it.

"You saved her," Tiffani says.

"We saved many."

She folds the slate. "And we'll save more. It will cost us."

He nods. "We'll pay it together."

"Together."

They sit shoulder to shoulder as crews finish another segment. No declarations. No sentiment.

Just structural truth held between them.

When the crew signals completion, they rise.

Time to continue.

***

# CHAPTER 12: “HOLDING TOGETHER” — DRAFT 3 CLEAN

Two Tides after the junction collapse, Saint Merro hums in a lower key as Quench phase settles over the district.

Not emergency noise now—no runners shouting, no constant clatter of crisis crews. Just measured reconstruction: hammers on brace-iron, quiet orders, carts rolling slow over newly patched seams. Heat rises in low waves from fresh welds. The air tastes of stone dust and forge-smoke.

Jhace sits on the overlook above the rebuilt forge, back against warm stone. His hands rest palm-up on his knees. The tremor moves through his fingers in continuous waves—Stage 3 baseline now, a new permanent rhythm etched into nerve and bone. Copper-gold tracery runs from palms to elbows, bright even without active Resonance.

Footsteps approach. Even gait, with the faint hitch he could pick out in a crowd.

Tiffani.

She sets two cups of water and a wrapped bundle between them. No greeting. No apology. She passes him a cup. He drinks. The water is mineral and heat and the taste of a city not currently falling.

She sits beside him, close enough that their shoulders could touch if either shifted a fraction closer. Below, crews finish securing a new lattice along one of the mid-corridor junction runs—seven braces in overlapping pattern, all angles and arcs. Her design. Their implementation.

“We’re planning the next phase,” she says. “Seven junction points tied into one reinforcement scheme. Distributed load. Two Anneals at minimum.” A beat. “Three, if we follow our own protocols instead of cheating them.”

His mouth twists. “Three, then.”

She watches him, measuring whether he means it. The tremor doesn’t let up. He doesn’t look away.

Silence settles again. Not empty—just full.

She unwraps the bundle. Bread. A narrow wedge of hard cheese. She breaks the bread, passes him the larger piece without comment. He accepts it without protest. She’s already calculated who needs more.

“There’s something you need to know,” she says.

He waits.

“Marisse,” she continues. “My friend. The glasshouse accident.”

He turns slightly toward her.

“Before the Piercing, I worked eastern-rim glasshouses,” she says. “Custom observatories. Thermal balances. Stress tests. We were trialing a new panel configuration—larger panes, different alloy ratio.” Her fingers press crumbs into a line on her knee, then smooth them away. “I ran the numbers. Checked them. Triple-checked them. Then told her it was safe.”

Quench light catches along the copper dust embedded in the overlook stone.

“The third pane shattered on heat swell,” she says. “Thermal expansion on that batch was higher than the standard coefficient. I used the Guild table instead of measuring that glass. That was the variable that mattered.” Her voice stays level. “Pane fractured. Rails twisted. The top segment came down. The shards cut deep. She didn’t survive to see the healers.”

He doesn’t answer with comfort. He just lets the words stand.

“I recalculated that configuration hundreds of times after,” she says. “The error is always the same. I trusted a number that was wrong and didn’t verify it.”

He studies her hands—still, but tight at the knuckles.

“How old were you?” he asks.

“Nineteen Turns.”

“Apprentice on an advanced commission.”

“Yes.”

He looks back out over the district. Lanterns are starting to appear in upper windows, pale in the thinning light.

“I was seven when my parents died,” he says. “Underface midline. Maintenance crew.”

The memory surfaces with unwelcome clarity. Stone under small bare feet. Damp metal smell. The grid hum steady in his bones.

“I was in the street above their sector,” he goes on. “Standing near an access hatch I wasn’t supposed to touch. I heard a crack. Not loud—just… wrong. Support shifting under strain.” His fingers curl against his knees, tremor shivering through the motion. “I thought about shouting. Or running to the foreman. Then I imagined being wrong. Them laughing. Saying I was making noise to avoid being sent home when the shift ended.”

His jaw tightens. “So I stayed quiet. Ten breaths later, their section collapsed.”

He remembers the dust plume punching up through the street seams. The way the hum dropped two notes and never quite returned to its old pitch in that sector.

“Everyone told me it wasn’t my fault,” he says. “I was a child. No training. Couldn’t have known.”

“But you believe you could have,” Tiffani says.

He nods once. “I heard it. They died. My mind writes that equation one way. My fear killed them.”

She watches the crews below for a long breath before speaking.

“We will spend our lives believing lies about things we couldn’t control,” she says quietly.

He huffs something that isn’t quite a laugh. “You’re calling my guilt bad math.”

“I’m calling mine bad math,” she says. “Yours mirrors it. Cleanly.”

He lets that sit. It fits uncomfortably well.

“So what then?” he asks. “We keep carrying it?”

“We carry it,” she says. “But we don’t have to let it define the structure.”

He glances at her. “What’s the alternative reference?”

“Each other,” she says.

He waits.

“You believe you killed them by not shouting,” she says. “I believe you were a child who heard a sound you couldn’t interpret yet. I believe your silence didn’t break that beam.”

“You weren’t there.”

“I don’t need to be. I understand the structure. The timing. The training you didn’t have.” Her gaze stays on him. “You believe you killed them. I believe you didn’t.”

“And Marisse?” he asks.

“I believe I made a wrong call,” she answers, without flinching. “I also believe the Guild table was flawed. Both things are true. If you told me it was only my fault, I’d know that was bad math.”

He turns that over slowly. “So we trade,” he says. “You hold the equation I can’t see straight. I hold yours.”

“That,” she says, “is load redistribution.”

Below, the crew chief signals completion—two short bell strikes. Junction four’s lattice officially enters service. The new braces hum at a slightly different pitch from the old stone, but the pattern is stable.

Tiffani pulls her slate into her lap. The surface is already crowded with lines and arcs. She clears a section with the side of her thumb.

“Next phase,” she says. “Seven junctions tied into a single reinforcement scheme. We can’t save every structure. So we prioritize the ones that carry the most secondary load.”

She sketches: nodes, paths, stress arrows.

“These three,” she marks with a sharp tap of chalk, “run over old repair seams. They suit your Wholeness. These two,” another symbol, “are geometry problems first. I take them. We overlap here and here.” She circles two junctions. “Redundancy. Neither of us is a single point of failure.”

He leans in, reading the pattern. “How long,” he asks, “if we respect the protocols?”

“Three Anneals,” she says. “If we don’t cheat on rest. If we keep cost at Stage 3 and not beyond.”

He looks at his hands, tremor steady, copper-gold lines bright. “Three it is.”

A familiar figure crosses the junction below. Chloen. No limp now. No brace. She carries a bundle of tools, stops to lay one hand along a new brace in quiet appreciation, then moves on toward the next site.

“You saved her,” Tiffani says.

“We saved her,” he corrects automatically.

She allows that with a faint incline of her head. “We save what we can,” she says. “We fail where we must. We miscalculate. We overextend. We pay.”

He watches his hands. The shake. The tracery. The reminder of that payment.

“Then we pay together,” he says.

“Together,” she echoes.

Quench deepens. Lanterns multiply. Workers drift toward food, toward whatever passes for rest between Tides. The newly braced junction hums steady—one secure node in a network they are slowly, deliberately teaching to hold.

Tiffani folds the slate. “We visit junction five next Tide. We should rest while rest is structurally available.”

“Structurally available,” he repeats, mouth twitching. “Not emotionally comfortable.”

“Emotional comfort is not one of our design constraints,” she says.

He exhales a breath that might almost be a laugh. “No. It isn’t.”

They stand. His tremor shakes the last crumbs from his fingers. Her leg catches once on the first step down; she adjusts before he can reach for her.

They descend from the overlook into a city still fractured, still dangerous, still theirs to brace where they can.

Not alone.

Not anymore.

Together, they walk toward whatever still needs holding.

# CHAPTER 13: “THE GRIP THAT HOLDS” — MODE B REFINED DRAFT

The aftershocks don’t come as single jolts anymore. They arrive as sequences—rolling waves translating through bedrock like the planet remembering it’s been pierced.

This one starts in early Kindling.

Jhace feels it in his teeth before the first structural gauge twitches. A low, thrumming wobble under the grid-hum, like a note trying to split the city’s pitch in two.

He’s at junction seven—one of the new lattice nodes they spent an entire Anneal planning. Braces gleam dull in the half-light, still too new to have collected city dust.

The first wave hits.

Stone underfoot slides a fraction sideways. The junction housing groans—a deep metal complaint that resonates in his ribs. Lattice bars flex, then hold.

“Load spike at seven,” Tiffani’s voice crackles through the signal pipe from the coordination point. “Reading’s up nine percent. Within tolerance.”

“For now,” Jhace mutters.

He lays both palms against the housing. The tremor in his hands is Stage 3 constant now—fine shaking even at rest—but his sensitivity to stress is sharper than it’s ever been.

The second wave comes harder.

The pressure rams through the grid, shunting load unevenly from a failing line somewhere downslope. Junction seven starts taking weight it was never meant to carry.

His hands pick up the change first: vibration sharpening, pitch flattening, tiny shifts in how the brace bolts sing against stone.

“Seven is overloading,” he says. “East lattice leg is picking up almost double projected.”

He doesn’t wait for Tiffani’s recalculation. The structure is moving now.

He moves with it.

Wholeness-bias flickers at the edges of his awareness, hungry to engage. He’s already sitting on Stage 3; any real push nudges him toward the edge of something he hasn’t named because he doesn’t want to.

“Jhace.” Her voice again, tighter this time. “We can bleed load into five and nine if you hold for sixty breaths. I’m mapping the new pattern.”

Sixty breaths with junction seven trying to shear loose.

He presses harder. Copper-gold heat pushes out along his palms, seeping into the housing. Cracks that hadn’t formed yet start to show themselves to his touch, each one a path for failure.

He seals what he can. Just enough. Never all.

The third wave arrives like a hammer.

The entire junction jerks. One of the east braces shrieks as the bolt anchoring it skips half a thread. The housing tilts a fraction.

His legs go with it. He slams one shoulder into the metal to keep himself upright.

“Seven is past safe,” he grits out. “We’re beyond the curve, Tiff.”

There’s a brief pause on the line. Calculation. Assessment.

“Protocols say release,” she says. “Let it fail. Save yourself for the next node.”

Forty-seven civilians shelter in the hab-block tied into junction seven. He counted them when they went in. Faces, not numbers.

“I’m not releasing,” he says.

“Jhace.”

“I can keep it.” He drags in a breath. “Just reroute as fast as you can.”

Wholeness-bias slams fully to the surface, as if it’s been waiting for that line. Copper-gold light threads up his arms and into the metal. Every place the housing is already tired, already micro-fractured, he fills with stubborn intent.

*Hold. You don’t get to fail yet.*

The grid obeys. For a moment.

Then the cost starts to land.

The tremor moves past Stage 3’s familiar territory into something worse—no longer a constant vibration, but rolling waves of misfiring muscle. His fingers clench and release without permission. His forearms buzz like struck wire. His heart stutters in his chest, then slams back into rhythm too hard.

“Jhace.” Tiffani’s voice again, sharper. “Talk to me.”

“Still here,” he forces out. “Still holding.”

“Your pattern is distorting the readings. I can’t see clean numbers.”

“Trust what I feel,” he says. “It’s not done trying to break yet.”

He can’t see the coordination point from here, but he knows exactly what she’s doing: scribbling updated load paths, redrawing the lattice in her head, trying to buy seconds without burning him out.

The fourth wave hits, not as a shove but as a grind. Everything leans, just a little. Enough to make the braces complain and the housing try to twist out of its anchors.

His knees start to slip.

*If I fall, the hold breaks. If the hold breaks, forty-seven people die in one long, crushing moment.*

His body offers collapse anyway. Muscles near the edge of seizure. Breath coming in short, ragged pulls. Copper-gold has climbed to his shoulders now; he can feel the tracery itching under his skin.

He tightens his jaw. Keeps his hands where they are.

“You’re past limits,” Tiffani says in his ear. “Protocol says you release at this stage.”

“Protocol didn’t hear that last wave,” he snarls through his teeth.

“What do you think I’m reading?” she snaps back. Then, softer: “I’m on my way.”

He doesn’t want her to run through aftershocks with a leg that still remembers being broken. He doesn’t have air to argue.

He holds.

Time loses shape. There’s only the pressure, the screaming metal, the way his bones feel like they’re transmitting every vibration straight into his skull. His vision narrows to the smear of copper-gold against corroded iron.

Somewhere in the roar, footsteps approach. Familiar cadence. Uneven, but fast.

Tiffani.

She appears at his side, breath high, hair pulled back messier than he’s seen it in any non-crisis Tide. She takes in the tilt, the braces, the glow crawling up his arms.

“You’re past Stage 3,” she says. No accusation. Just measurement.

“Can’t—” His jaw spasms. “Can’t drop.”

“You will,” she says. “But not alone.”

She steps in behind him, bracing herself so her weight supports his center of gravity. Her hands come down over his on the housing—her fingers lacing through his, skin over copper tracery, her grip steady where his shakes.

“I’m here,” she says into his ear. “Hold.”

Something in his chest that has been pulled like wire since he was seven unknots a fraction at those words.

He’s still the one interfacing with the metal. Still the channel for Wholeness-bias. But her presence backs the demand he’s making of reality. She is not adding force to the housing. She is adding *force to him*.

On the coordination pipe, someone shouts about new brace placements. Tiffani ignores it. All her focus is on the way his hands keep trying to twitch away from their points and how her fingers keep them locked.

“Listen,” she says quietly. “I’m going to count you through the next wave. Four in, hold, four out, like before.”

Another wave is coming. He can feel it building in the grid, in his own bones.

“Breathe with me,” she says. “Now. Four in.”

He drags air in as the next surge hits.

He doesn’t break.

“Hold.”

The grid shudders around them. The junction housing wails like something alive being asked to carry too much.

“Four out.”

He exhales as the wave peaks and begins to pass.

Again. Again. Each cycle, the panic at the edges of his vision eases just enough to keep him from going under. His heart still hammers at wrong tempos, his muscles still threaten to seize, but the breathing pattern holds him steady in the center of all that chaos.

Guild crews arrive. Bracing timbers slam into place under Tiffani’s shouted direction. She’s reading the structure through his hands now—feeling where stillness wants to fail, barking coordinates without looking away from the copper-gold glow.

“Rear plate, left seam—brace there. No, higher. There. Good. Cross-brace that. Now the under-run—yes.”

“Primary braces in!” someone calls. “Secondary locked!”

The load shifts perceptibly. Some of the crushing pressure leaves his bones and transfers into wood and iron and stone.

“On three,” Tiffani says quietly into his shoulder. “You let go. We both do. The braces take it. Not us.”

Fear flashes through him—irrational, but loud. *If I let go, they die. If I let go, I fail them like I failed—*

Her fingers tighten. “Then I hold you,” she says. “And you hold the junction. Together. That’s the only way this works.”

He closes his eyes.

“Three,” she says. “Two. One.”

They release.

For half a breath, the housing sags. Braces creak. Dust sifts down in a fine veil.

Then the structure catches.

The junction holds on engineered support.

Jhace’s knees give completely.

Tiffani goes down with him on purpose, redirecting the fall so they hit the wall instead of open stone. His whole body shakes now—full Stage 3+ event, muscles firing in chaotic waves. His fingers won’t uncurl; his hands still think they’re holding something.

She pries one hand open, then the other, and keeps both wrapped in hers.

“Four counts in,” she says.

He gasps.

“Try again.”

He does. Air in. Four counts. Barely.

“Hold.”

He holds.

“Four counts out.”

They repeat until his breathing no longer sounds like someone drowning in dry air. The tremor doesn’t stop, but it downgrades from catastrophic to merely unignorable.

“Look at me,” she says.

He does. It takes effort.

“You almost crossed into Stage 4,” she says. “You understand that, yes?”

He nods, motion jerky.

“You don’t get many of those,” she says. “Maybe none. And if you fall, I lose you and the city loses everything you can still hold at Stage 3.”

“I know,” he says. His voice comes out shredded.

“Do you?” she asks. “Or do you still believe you’re only useful when you’re breaking yourself?”

He doesn’t answer.

She threads her fingers through his shaking ones again, firmer this time. “We are load-bearing systems,” she says. “You said it first. Remove one, the other fails. You don’t get to make unilateral collapse decisions anymore.”

His mouth twitches at that. “Unilateral collapse decisions?”

“Yes.” Her tone softens—barely. “If you are going to destroy yourself, you’re going to do it by protocol, not impulse.”

A breath that might be a laugh lurches through him. “That…does not sound better.”

“It is,” she says. “Because it means I’m there when you do it. And if I’m there, maybe we find a different option.”

The crews finish securing the last brace. Lattice seven hums under new load—different tone, but stable. No more civilians in that branch are in immediate danger. For now.

Tiffani pushes herself to her feet, then hauls him up with her, taking more of his weight than she should with a leg that still remembers being shattered. He lets her.

His hands still won’t quite stop shaking. Copper-gold has climbed higher along his arms now, disappearing under the sleeves of his shirt. Cost made visible, and not just to him.

“We should rest,” she says.

“We should,” he agrees. “We’ll probably walk to the next junction instead.”

“Yes,” she says. “We probably will.”

They stand for one more breath, both facing the braced housing.

The grid hums. The city holds. Two people who are already paying more than they should choose, again, to keep paying.

Then they move on. Together.

Behind them, junction seven stays upright—carried now by braces and stone, not by a single pair of shaking hands.

The grip that holds has changed.

It isn’t just his anymore.

# CHAPTER 13: "THE GRIP THAT HOLDS" (TITLE CHAPTER) — MODE B REFINED DRAFT

Kindling phase opens with motion beneath the district—deep, rolling pulses transmitted through older stone. Not a singular aftershock but a sequence. Jhace feels it through his teeth before structural sensors register.

He reaches junction seven as the lattice network begins to misbehave.

Not failure—yet. But three nodes divert load into wrong vectors, distributing stress toward a single brace instead of across six. Pressure climbs past tolerance. Metal shifts from low compliance to high warning.

Below the junction: civilians still evacuating.

Tiffani's voice carries across the elevated corridor. "Anchor points two and five stable. Seven showing anomaly. Confirm?"

He places both hands to the housing. Vibration conducts wrong—sharp through forearms. "East brace carrying seventy instead of forty. Load's stacking."

"Release and move to alternate pattern. We redirect from here."

He runs the numbers. Thirty seconds to release, forty-five to reposition, longer for recalibration. Tiffani can correct the pattern, but civilian threshold time is shorter.

"I'm holding," he says.

"Jhace—"

"I'm holding."

He drives Wholeness-bias into fracture lines. Copper-gold spreads through metal, saturating weakened seams. The tremor reacts—Stage 3 baseline detonates upward. Shoulders lock. Jaw clenches. Vision contracts.

The structure steadies.

Tension doesn't ease.

Aftershock sequence intensifies. Load redistributes *onto* his hold instead of away.

Footsteps approach—Tiffani's uneven gait, brace clicking.

She reaches him. Studies the junction in two breaths. "This needs coordinated release. You cannot hold primary load alone."

"No time."

"Then we make time." She positions herself to his left. "Crew is two junctions away. When I say release, you shift forty percent load. I catch it with Form-bias. Then you reanchor."

He nods—the only movement he can spare.

Her hands settle on adjacent joints. No Soulpulse yet—just contact. Mapping geometry.

"Three. Two. One. Now."

He releases partial load.

She activates. Precise geometric pulse intercepts distributed force, redirecting vectors into secondary joints. Metal strains but holds.

"Reset. Anchor at fifty-five."

He anchors. Pressure eases by a fraction—survivable but dangerous.

"Guild ETA?" he manages.

"Three minutes."

Vision narrows—edges greying. Aftershock resonance cycles faster. His body pushes toward Stage 4.

She shifts behind him, covers his hands with hers—skin over copper-gold, stabilizing pressure.

"Then I hold you," she says quietly. "And you hold the junction. Together."

Something steadies internally—not muscles, not tremor, but the falling sensation that precedes collapse.

"I'm here," she says. "Hold."

He holds.

Guild crew arrives. Bracing timbers slot under worst joints. Reinforcement bars lock into place.

"Secure," the crew lead calls.

"Release on three," Tiffani says. "One. Two. Three."

They release.

The junction holds—stone and timber, engineering and time, not Soulpulse.

Jhace drops to his knees. Tiffani catches him—barely—and guides him down against the wall.

His tremor is catastrophic: full-body, uncontrolled, Stage 3 collapse state brushing Stage 4's threshold. Copper-gold traces extend past elbows now—permanently etched.

She holds his wrists. "Four counts in."

He tries. Breath hitches at three.

"Again. Four counts."

He succeeds.

They repeat until breathing stabilizes, though tremor remains severe.

He stares at his hands. Copper-gold bright even without Resonance. "This is permanent."

"Yes."

"I can't work like this."

"You will adapt."

"What if I can't?"

She meets his eyes. "Then I carry you. The same way you've carried me through every failure I couldn't calculate. That's partnership."

He absorbs this. The tremor doesn't ease.

Tiffani helps him stand. Movement is slow—his coordination compromised, her leg still damaged.

They face the corridor ahead.

The work continues. The cost continues.

They continue.

Behind them, junction seven stands fully braced—stable because of engineered reinforcement, not emergency endurance.

A structure that will last.

A partnership that does the same.

# CHAPTER 13: *“THE GRIP THAT HOLDS”* — DRAFT 3 CLEAN (HYBRID)

Aftershocks don’t come as single jolts anymore. They arrive as sequences—rolling waves remembering the spindle went straight through the world.

This one starts in Kindling.

Jhace feels it in his teeth before any gauge twitches. The grid-hum under Saint Merro wobbles off its normal pitch, a low, unstable beat under everything else.

He’s at junction seven—one of the new lattice nodes he and Tiffani spent an Anneal designing. Braces sit too clean against old stone, not yet dust-softened by the city.

The first wave hits.

Stone slides a fraction sideways. The junction housing groans—a deep, metal sound that lands in his ribs. Lattice bars flex, then hold.

On the signal pipe above him, Tiffani’s voice comes through tight but controlled. “Anchor points two and five stable. Seven showing anomaly. Confirm?”

Jhace lays both palms against the housing. Stage 3 tremor makes his fingers shake even at rest, but sensitivity is all edge now; every vibration has shape.

Vibration here feels wrong—sharp, overfocused. “East brace is carrying seventy instead of forty. Load’s stacking into this node.”

“Releasing here and moving to alternate pattern,” she says. “We can bleed into five and nine if you let seven float.”

He runs the numbers, fast and ugly. Breaths to release. Breaths to reposition. Breaths before the overstressed seam lets go.

Below the junction, forty-seven civilians shelter in the hab-block tied into this branch.

“Not enough time,” he says. “I’m holding.”

“Jhace—”

“I’m holding.”

He drives Wholeness-bias into every fracture his hands can find. Copper-gold light threads out along the housing seams, filling microcracks, stiffening tired metal.

The structure steadies.

The tension does not.

The second wave comes harder. Somewhere downslope a line fails, and the lattice does what all networks do under sudden change—it pushes the problem to its strongest point.

Junction seven.

Pressure piles onto his hold instead of away from it. One of the east bolts shrieks as it skips half a thread. The housing tilts a measurable degree.

His knees go with it. He jams one shoulder against the casing to stop himself sliding.

“Seven’s past safe,” he grits. “We’re beyond the curve, Tiff.”

There’s a pause on the pipe. Calculation.

“Protocol says release,” she says. “Let it fail. Save yourself for the next node.”

He can picture them. Forty-seven people under reinforced stone, trusting the grid to carry their weight.

“I’m not releasing,” he says.

“Jhace.”

“I can keep it. Reroute as fast as you can.”

Wholeness-bias answers his refusal like it’s been waiting. Copper-gold climbs up his forearms, etching bright lines under skin. Every point of weakness pulls at him for attention; he floods more into them than he should.

His tremor stops feeling like tremor and starts feeling like misfire—muscles firing out of rhythm, jaw locking without his input, heart stuttering and slamming.

“Your pattern’s distorting the readings,” Tiffani snaps through the pipe. “I can’t get clean numbers.”

“Trust what I feel,” he forces out. “It’s not done trying to break yet.”

Another wave builds. He can sense it coming—not in stone this time, but in his own bones.

On the pipe: “I’m coming to you.”

He doesn’t want her running through aftershocks on a leg that still remembers shattering. He doesn’t have breath to argue.

He holds.

Time smears into vibration and copper-gold. The junction wails in metal language—*too much, too much*—and he answers with the same word he’s been giving failing structures since he was old enough to see a crack.

*Hold.*

Footsteps pound closer. Uneven, but fast.

“Tilt three degrees east,” Tiffani says as she rounds the last bend, already seeing what he feels. “Bolt skip at the second brace.”

She takes in the glow climbing his arms, the way his shoulders jerk with each new surge, the slight lag in his eyes when he drags them to her.

“You’re past Stage 3,” she says. Not accusation. Measurement.

“Can’t…” His jaw spasms. “Can’t drop.”

“You will,” she says. “But not alone.”

She moves in behind him, planting her feet where his balance is weakest. Her hands come down over his, fingers lacing through his around corroded iron. Her grip is steady where his shakes.

“I’m here,” she says quietly, right against his ear. “Hold.”

Something in his chest that’s been wound tight since childhood loosens half a turn at the words.

On the pipe, someone calls brace positions. Tiffani ignores it. Her focus narrows to the way his hands keep trying to twitch off their points and how hers keep them anchored.

“Listen,” she says, voice tight but precise. “Next wave, we breathe together. Four in, hold, four out. You know the pattern.”

He feels the surge building. Grid hum dropping toward a note it shouldn’t hit.

“Now,” she says. “Four in.”

He drags air into rebel lungs as the next wave hits.

He doesn’t break.

“Hold.”

The junction lurches. Braces squeal. For a heartbeat it feels like the entire district is hanging off his ruined arms.

“Four out.”

He exhales as the surge crests and rolls past.

Again. Again. Each cycle, the panic at the edges of his vision thins just enough for him to keep demanding what he’s demanding of the metal.

Around them, Guild crews slam bracing timbers into place, moving to the coordinates she barks without taking her hands off his.

“Rear plate, left seam—there. Cross-brace higher. Good. Under-run next. Faster.”

“Primary braces in!” someone calls. “Secondary locked!”

The load shifts.

Some of the crushing pressure lifts from his bones and settles into wood and stone where it belongs.

“On three,” she says into his shoulder. “We release. Braces take it. Not us.”

Fear spikes—old, irrational, loud. *If I let go, they die. If I let go, I fail them like before—*

Her fingers tighten painfully around his. “Then I hold you,” she says. “And you hold the junction. Together.”

He closes his eyes.

“One,” she says. “Two. Three.”

They release.

The housing sags for half a breath. Braces groan. Dust releases in a fine, choking veil.

Then the structure catches.

Junction seven holds. Not on his hands. On the lattice they built and the bracing crews that listened.

Jhace’s knees go out completely.

Tiffani goes down with him on purpose, twisting so they hit the wall instead of open stone. His whole body shakes now—full Stage 3 event pushed toward the edge of something worse. His fingers stay curled like they’re still gripping metal.

She peels them open one at a time and keeps both wrapped in hers.

“Four counts in,” she says.

He gasps.

“Again. Four.”

He manages it. Barely.

“Hold.”

He holds.

“Four out.”

They repeat until his breathing sounds human instead of like someone drowning without water. The tremor doesn’t stop. It downgrades—from catastrophic to constant, from immediate emergency to permanent condition.

“Look at me,” she says.

He does. It takes effort.

“You almost crossed into Stage 4,” she says. “You understand that?”

He nods. The motion jerks.

“You don’t get many shots at that,” she continues. “Maybe none. If you fall, I lose you. The city loses everything you can still do at Stage 3.”

“I know,” he rasps.

“Do you?” Her gaze doesn’t soften. “Or do you still think you’re only useful when you’re breaking yourself?”

He doesn’t answer.

She threads her fingers more firmly through his shaking ones—an anchor, not comfort. “We are load-bearing systems,” she says. “You said it first. Remove one, the other fails. You don’t get to make unilateral collapse decisions anymore.”

His mouth twitches. “Unilateral…collapse decisions?”

“Yes.” A fraction of dryness enters her tone. “If you destroy yourself, you will at least do it with me present. By protocol. Not impulse.”

A ragged breath almost becomes a laugh. “That does not sound better.”

“It is,” she says. “Because if I’m there, maybe we find another option. And if we don’t”—her grip tightens—“then I carry you. The same way you’ve carried me through every failure I couldn’t calculate.”

His eyes drop to their joined hands. Copper-gold has climbed past his elbows now, disappearing under his sleeves. Her fingers are dusted with the same light.

“Then I hold you,” she says, “and you hold the junction. Together.”

He closes his eyes once. Opens them again.

“Together,” he says.

The crews finish securing the last brace. Junction seven hums under new load—a different note, but stable. The branch below stays intact. Civilians live their unremarkable lives, never knowing how close they came to being crushed in the dark.

Tiffani pushes herself upright, then hauls him to his feet, taking more of his weight than her leg should allow. He lets her.

His hands still won’t stop shaking.

“We should rest,” she says.

“We should,” he agrees. “We’ll probably walk to the next junction instead.”

“Yes,” she answers. “We probably will.”

They stand for one more breath, facing the braced housing. The grid hums. The city holds. Two people who are already paying more than they should choose, again, to keep paying.

Then they move on.

Behind them, junction seven stays upright—held by lattice and bracing, not by a single pair of hands.

The grip that holds the district is no longer his alone.

---
